q:
This board means: end of all local prohibitions imposed on vehicles in motion.

A. Right.
B. Wrong.
e:
This is sign C 46. It is a prohibition sign.
Example: imagine you see a traffic sign saying that the maximum speed allowed is 30 km/h. Then that sign will apply until the next intersection or until you see this sign, or a road sign that imposes another maximum speed limit.

q:
An ordinary road divided into two lanes in Wallonia.
You are driving this car at 110 km/h. The police stop you.
What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. Your driving licence is immediately and temporarily withdrawn.
C. You will have to explain yourself in court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence if you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
If you drive in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area more than 20 km/h too fast, you also risk an immediate withdrawal.
This immediate withdrawal is done by the police, because it is a serious offence.
For any serious offence, your driving licence can be immediately withdrawn.
clean_q:

What are the consequences?

C. You will have to explain yourself in court.
clean_e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence if you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
If you drive in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area more than 20 km/h too fast, you also risk an immediate withdrawal.
This immediate withdrawal is done by the police, because it is a serious offence.
For any serious offence, your driving licence can be immediately withdrawn.

q:
You are driving at 47 km/h in a residential area in Wallonia. What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence.
C. You will have to appear before the court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone, or a residential area (woonerf), you already risk an immediate withdrawal if you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a disqualification from driving as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not decided by the police but by the judge when your case is handled in court.
clean_q:
You are driving at 47 km/h in a residential area in Wallonia. What are the consequences?
A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence. 
C. You will have to appear before the court.
clean_e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone, or a residential area (woonerf), you already risk an immediate withdrawal if you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a disqualification from driving as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not decided by the police but by the judge when your case is handled in court.

q:
The police stop you in a built-up area in Flanders because you were driving at 75 km/h. What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence.
C. You will have to appear before the court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
But in built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already risk this when you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a driving disqualification as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is decided by the judge, not by the police.
clean_q:
The police stop you in a built-up area in Flanders because you were driving at 75 km/h. What are the consequences?
A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence. 
C. You will have to appear before the court.
clean_e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
But in built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already risk this when you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a driving disqualification as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is decided by the judge, not by the police.

q:
The police stopped you because you were driving at 67 km/h in a built-up area in the Brussels region.
What are the consequences?

A. You only have to pay a fine.
B. Your driving licence will be temporarily withdrawn.
C. You will have to appear in court.
e:
LESSON 12
The maximum speed limit in a built-up area in the Brussels region is 30 km/h.
You were therefore driving 36 km/h too fast.
A driving disqualification is imposed when you exceed the limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already risk disqualification if you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not done by the police but by a judge in court.
Depending on the verdict, you may lose your right to drive for at least 8 days and up to 5 years, even for vehicles that do not require a driving licence.
For novice drivers (with a full driving licence for less than 2 years), the rule is even stricter.
clean_q:
The police stopped you because you were driving at 67 km/h in a built-up area in the Brussels region.
What are the consequences?
A. You only have to pay a fine.
B. Your driving licence will be temporarily withdrawn.
C. You will have to appear in court.
clean_e:
LESSON 12
The maximum speed limit in a built-up area in the Brussels region is 30 km/h.
You were therefore driving 36 km/h too fast.
A driving disqualification is imposed when you exceed the limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already risk disqualification if you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not done by the police but by a judge in court.
Depending on the verdict, you may lose your right to drive for at least 8 days and up to 5 years, even for vehicles that do not require a driving licence.
For novice drivers (with a full driving licence for less than 2 years), the rule is even stricter.

q: Can you overtake a cyclist on the right with a car on an elevated section?
e: For years, it has been allowed to overtake a cyclist on the right who is about to turn left. Since 2023, overtaking a cyclist on the left is also allowed.

q:
What is the maximum speed limit on this road within a built-up area in Brussels Region after driving over the raised surface?

A. 30 km/hour.
B. 50 km/hour.
C. 70 km/hour.
e:
LESSON 12 – ‘Speed’.
On the speed hump: 30 km/hour.
Within a built-up area: 50 km/hour in Flanders and Wallonia. In Brussels Region: 30 kph.

LESSON 12 – ‘Speed’.
The maximum speed permitted on ordinary roads outside built-up areas =
in Flanders: 70 km/hour.
in Wallonia and Brussels region: 90 km/hour.
Nowhere is it forbidden to drive 70 km/hour.
